tweets.text:
One of my favorite lines from Gavin is "if you're going to do a hard thing, do the whole thing."

Etched has done some genuinely impressive things to vertically integrate.

They own a factory in Taiwan. Built a two-megawatt data center in their office and cloned test stations for 24/7 cycles across both sites.

"We want to vertically integrate the entire product as much as possible, both because we get more performance and because we can move way faster.

We're the only startup right now building its own rack as well as its own chips, and we did it all at the same time.

We went through multiple iterations of the rack before the chips even came back.

We made thermal chips with the exact same hotspots we expected, so we could build the cold plates, over-pressurize them, and blow them up.

We haven't had a single leak since our real chips came back, because we'd already validated everything.

We have a factory in Taiwan. We built a clone of a bunch of the test stations in our office.

We have a two-megawatt data center on this floor. We did 24/7 development cycles.

It's that extreme vertical integration and extreme parallelization of the schedule that lets you get products to market way faster."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Three years ago, two Harvard dropouts set out to build a better AI chip than the largest companies in the world. 

Almost everyone I called at the time said it was impossible.

Today, Etched (@Etched) comes out of stealth with $800M total raised, $1B in signed customer contracts, https://t.co/ezMrqaIEfS

tweets.text:
I have argued about this with friends. Some believe that I overestimate the return of Third-Worldism as an ideology, and they insist that voters care about material issues such as wages, jobs, and housing. They are not wrong. Material precarity is real, it becomes politically dangerous when inequality remains unaddressed, and a nation should worry when its young people cannot work, cannot form families, and cannot plan for their future. However, however, however, and however, the political energy that is now forming is not purely economic. It blends genuine social grievances with an ideological reflex rooted in post-colonial and anti-imperial narratives.

https://t.co/ecxa1Qyvxa

tweets.text:
People in crypto forget how insane the tech actually is.

You can send $500K to someone in Indonesia from Canada in 10 seconds for less than a cent in fees.

No middleman approvals, wire fees, or business days.

That is the real bull case for crypto.

It is simply a better way to transact.

tweets.text:
I just talked to another investor about this startup. I asked how fundraising went for them.

"He started his fundraising calls at 9:00 and he was done by 10:30."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: To get an accurate estimate you have to multiply by 3 or 4 to account for dilution by the time the company is worth billions. But the implied probability is still usually ridiculously low. Yesterday I talked to a startup whose implied probability was 1/10,000.
tweets.full_text:
I just talked to another investor about this startup. I asked how fundraising went for them.

"He started his fundraising calls at 9:00 and he was done by 10:30."

tweets.text:
Everyone trying to find reasons to FUD solana:CARDSccUMFKoPRZxt5vt3ksUbxEFEcnZ3H2pd3dKxYjp for the engagement farm.

In the meantime, @Collector_Crypt is posting new monthly ATHs across the board:

- Volume grew 107.6% MoM from $198M to $411M
- Gross volume grew 106.9% MoM from $102M to $211M
- Net revenue grew 74.4% MoM from $9M to $15.7M
- Daily active users grew 71.1% MoM too

Annualizing June numbers, we are talking about a business with $188M net revenue.

Applying this to solana:CARDSccUMFKoPRZxt5vt3ksUbxEFEcnZ3H2pd3dKxYjp valuations, this is a P/E multiple of 0.44x to circulating mcap, 2.52x to FDV

I think these numbers speak for themselves, so the big question you should ask yourself is:

Do you want to farm likes on the timeline, or do you want to make money?

tweets.text: Most boxing trainers use the pool noodles. It’s not unusual. HOWEVER, the trainer is smacking the noodles into her punches to make them seem way crisper than they are. tweets.text:
#uranium #Athabasca #mining

Is this the most impressive project portfolio in the Atabasca basin? Skyharbour Resources $SYH $SYH.V has built a portfolio with multiple shots on goal.

Drilling is now underway at the Russell Lake Project, where Skyharbour owns 80% and Denison Mines is funding its full pro-rata share of the next C$10M.

That's just one part of a planned 30,000+ metre drill season across six projects in 2026.

In my view, this is exactly how you build long-term shareholder value: combine aggressive self-funded exploration with partner-funded programs to maximize discovery potential while preserving capital.

With drill results expected over the coming months, Q3 and Q4 could be very interesting for Skyharbour.

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Skyharbour Resources. Not financial advice. Always do your own due diligence.

That's just one part of a planned 30,000+ metre drill season across six projects in 2026.

In my view, this is exactly how you build long-term shareholder value: combine aggressive self-funded exploration with partner-funded programs to maximize discovery potential while preserving capital.

With drill results expected over the coming months, Q3 and Q4 could be very interesting for Skyharbour.

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Skyharbour Resources. Not financial advice. Always do your own due diligence.

tweets.text:
#potash #lithium #mining

One thing that stands out about American Critical Minerals $KCLI $APCOF is where its Green River Project sits.

It's not an isolated exploration story. It's positioned in the heart of Utah's Paradox Basin, surrounded by projects that have already demonstrated the value of the basin.

• To the north and south, Anson Resources has advanced the same lithium brine system to DFS, with POSCO now funding a demonstration plant.

• To the east, Intrepid Potash has been commercially mining the same Paradox Formation since 1963.

KCLI controls the ground between these projects.

The upcoming seven-hole drill program isn't exploring an unproven basin, it's testing whether the same geological systems extend across American Critical Minerals' land package.

If drilling confirms the continuity of those neighboring mineralized horizons, the market may begin assigning significantly greater value to Green River.

With exposure to potash, lithium and bromine, and drilling expected in Q3, American Critical Minerals is approaching what could become its most important catalyst to date.

Disclosure: Paid partnership with American Critical Minerals. Not financial advice. Always do your own due diligence.

tweets.text:
Robotics is crypto's most obvious blind spot.

In 2016 it wasn't even a category investors tracked. Now it's the 2nd biggest in private markets, ahead of fintech, at $263B. Q1 alone did $16B across almost 500 deals.

Here's what almost nobody in crypto is pricing in...all that capital is about to hit a wall the traditional financial stack can't get past.

A robot can't open a bank account. It can't hold a legal identity, sign a contract, or get paid through ACH. Every rail in TradFi assumes a human sits behind the transaction. That works when a person signs for a few thousand machines. It breaks when millions of them start transacting on their own.

Robotics routes through crypto because crypto is the only stack ever built for non-human actors:

• Permissionless identity a machine can hold without a passport or a corporation
• Programmable payments that settle machine to machine, no bank in the loop
• Machine-readable ownership, so an asset can belong to something that isn't a person
• Coordination between autonomous agents with no legal entity in the middle

You could always make the argument Robotics doesn't need crypto. 

But I've always focused on following where the world's attention and hype lies, and Crypto tends to find a way of monetizing that mindshare. 

Where I'm looking

Here's who's building those rails, grouped by the layer of the stack they're solving. 

Not a buy list btw...just a watchlist of which piece of the machine economy each one is going after.

• Coordination. Virtuals (@virtuals_io) runs the biggest AI agent ecosystem and is now pushing those agents into the physical world. Its Eastworld robotics track covers teleop data, simulation, and real robot deployment. 50k+ agents recently got access to 430+ tokenized equities through Ondo / Treasures.

• Location. Robots are useless without precise positioning, and GEODNET (@GEODNET) supplies it. 21k+ stations across 150+ countries, roughly $6.7M in annualized revenue and $5.3M in annualized holder revenue through buybacks and burns.

• Identity and payments. Fabric (@FabricFND) handles the backend: machine identity, wallets, payments, and task coordination. Plumbing for a world where machines transact onchain without a human in the loop.

• Telemetry. IoTeX (@iotex_io) has been grinding on DePIN and machine data for years. Device identity, verifiable offchain compute, real-world machine data. Not flashy, but it's what matters when robots need telemetry anyone can trust.

• Spatial. Auki (@AukiLabs) is building Posemesh, a spatial awareness network that helps robots, smart glasses, and other devices understand the physical spaces around them.

• Ownership. xMAQUINA (@xmaquina) wraps physical AI exposure in a DAO. Fractional machine ownership and robotics deals that normally sit behind VC rounds, backed by an $18M treasury with $7.6M set aside for robotics investments.

• Simulation. Strike (@StrikeRobot_ai) works on humanoid autonomy and sim-to-real training. They recently rebuilt a real Eastworlds lab inside a simulation to test whether behaviors trained in sim survive contact with the real world.

• Training data. Caspius (@caspius_ai) is after embodied AI data. Robots learn from real-world task footage, and Caspius just hit an ATH in footage contributed while adding NFT/account pairing.

• Tooling. Roba (@Roba_Labs) wants to make robotics easier to build around. No-code tools, simulation, robot templates, a creator marketplace, and compute for training and inference.

The catch

I'm not going to pretend this is a rotation you front-run next week. Most of these are early, thinly traded, and completely dependent on physical AI actually scaling. If robots stall, these rails don't matter yet.

But the capital is already moving, and it's moving toward a category that structurally needs crypto rails to work. I'd rather map this corner early than chase it once it's obvious.

tweets.text:
The hardest part of macro isnt the view. Its the trade. So thematic baskets exist for exactly this... rates lower, the Exponential Age, the dollar topping. Pick the view, get the basket, watch it run. 

This is how you trade macro without having to be a macro trader. And the price you come in at before 4 July is the one you keep.

tweets.text:
For instance, if you want to have a healthy life, you shouldn’t have twelve sausage links and a beer every day for breakfast. In other words, you need to constantly connect and reconcile the data you’re gathering at different levels in order to draw a complete picture of what’s going on. Like synthesizing in general, some people are naturally better at this than others, but anyone can learn to do this to one degree or another.
#principleoftheday

tweets.text:
The most retarded part of the panic dump of $STRC paired against BTC has now been fully retraced

Unless you think that STRC should trade much lower *even* with BTC at $60k, then the STRC/BTC ratio dumping suggests market irrationality because BTC should be a higher beta on the way down

Several other factors could influence this ratio and it's not that straightforward, but overall I think it's a correct mental framework

tweets.text:
SATA and STRC are trying to strip out the volatility from a pref like STRF by adjusting the yield instead of the price, but the market is adjusting STRF yield every minute by moving its price, compared to the SATA yield which is adjusting much more slowly. This means that the volatility needs to "go somewhere" until you're adjusting the yield again, and given the volatility of STRF you should probably expect SATA to trade in a 20% range roughly, for example from $90 to $110. If you're absorbing all the upside volatility above $100, then you're most likely lowering the floor of the 20% range, so instead of having it trade between $90 and $110 you'll have it trade between $80 and $100

(On top of letting shorts have a free ride + cutting all upside above $100 for holders + selling at $100 something that people would be ready to buy at higher price)

All this to say that your decision is correct, but I think even under 'normal conditions' you shouldn't let the market expect a perma ATM at $100!
tweets.full_text:
SATA and STRC are trying to strip out the volatility from a pref like STRF by adjusting the yield instead of the price, but the market is adjusting STRF yield every minute by moving its price, compared to the SATA yield which is adjusting much more slowly. This means that the volatility needs to "go somewhere" until you're adjusting the yield again, and given the volatility of STRF you should probably expect SATA to trade in a 20% range roughly, for example from $90 to $110. If you're absorbing all the upside volatility above $100, then you're most likely lowering the floor of the 20% range, so instead of having it trade between $90 and $110 you'll have it trade between $80 and $100

(On top of letting shorts have a free ride + cutting all upside above $100 for holders + selling at $100 something that people would be ready to buy at higher price)

All this to say that your decision is correct, but I think even under 'normal conditions' you shouldn't let the market expect a perma ATM at $100!

tweets.text:
You can't bluntly compare the dividend rate on prefs to the perp funding rate because the funding rate is proportional to the price of BTC whereas the dividend rate on prefs doesn't compound with the price of BTC. It's like if you locked in a funding rate at the price of BTC at year Y and then kept paying the total funding cost from year Y even on year Y+4 or even Y+10

In the short-term, they are very comparable, but in the long-term, they diverge significantly, which is precisely the mechanism that would enable outperformance vs BTC. Obviously, the whole thesis of Saylor is that BTC is going to compound at a high CAGR (20%+), so borrowing at a non-compounding 12% rate makes a lot of sense with that assumption in mind. But if you expect BTC to be flat for 5 years, then taking any leverage is by definition bad, whether it's 12% or 4% tbh

tweets.text:
The problem is that people want DAOs to democratise decision making where multiple disjoint groups are pivotable in the vote, including votes from people with no financial upside or stake in their decisions. 

DAOs, as currently implemented, do not enable the above goals. It is token weighted voting, where the goal is to encourage all large token holders to participate and vote. There might be some tokenholders significantly larger than others, but that’s how the system is intended to work. 

So the upset is arguably the mismatch between the vision (democratised voting) and the reality of weighted voting. 

The solution isn’t to attack a large tokenholder, especially since it is the founder of the project, for deciding to make governance more competitive and voting according to their own stake. 

Either DAOs need to design systems that do indeed democratise votes where no single actor can have the deciding vote. Or they should lean into the properties of token weighted voting and make voting a competitive landscape. 

We know the latter works. Nearly all large companies use weighted voting systems and they are worth trillions of dollars. There is a lot we can learn from the old legacy world.

So the upset is arguably the mismatch between the vision (democratised voting) and the reality of weighted voting. 

The solution isn’t to attack a large tokenholder, especially since it is the founder of the project, for deciding to make governance more competitive and voting according to their own stake. 

Either DAOs need to design systems that do indeed democratise votes where no single actor can have the deciding vote. Or they should lean into the properties of token weighted voting and make voting a competitive landscape. 

We know the latter works. Nearly all large companies use weighted voting systems and they are worth trillions of dollars. There is a lot we can learn from the old legacy world.
